segments.s568-g81: پراندیشه is a predicate adjective meaning 'full of thought/worried'; شد is the past-tense of شدن 'to become' indicating a change of state. جان شاپور شاه is the affected entity: جان ('soul/heart') with its possessor Shapur (شاپور) and his title شاه ('king') following it in poetic order, i.e. 'the soul/heart of Shapur the king.'
segments.s568-g82: که introduces an indirect question/clause ('wondering that/what'); فردا is a temporal adverb 'tomorrow' modifying the clause; چه is the interrogative 'what' and precedes the verb; سازد is the 3rd-person singular present (classical present used for future) 'will do' or 'does'; کنیزک پگاه is the subject phrase 'the little maid of (the) dawn' placed after the verb in poetic word order, with پگاه ('dawn/early morning') specifying the maid.
segments.s568-g73: ببست is a past 3rd-person singular (classical/poetic) form meaning 'he closed / fastened' used metaphorically here. اندر + اندیشه is a classical prepositional phrase 'in/into thought'; دل را is the definite object (را marks 'the heart') placed before the verb, and نخست 'first' adverbially scopes the whole clause. Word order is typical of older Persian: prepositional phrase + object + verb.
segments.s568-g74: از marks source ('from/out of'), آخور 'manger/trough' names the source, دو اسپ is numeral + noun ('two steeds/horses'), and گرانمایه is a postpositive adjective 'precious/esteemed' modifying اسپ. جست is a past tense (3rd sing.) of a classical verb meaning 'leapt/drew out' — here 'drew out/caused to emerge'. The verb comes at the end as usual in Persian.
segments.s568-g75: همان is a demonstrative/determiner ('that/the same') applied to the following list. تیغ 'sword' is the first noun, and the conjunction و links the subsequent items (گوپال and برگستوان). This is a nominal listing phrase with no verb in the segment — it continues the catalogue of equipment.
segments.s568-g76: Again همان ('that/the same') introduces another grouped item. جوشن 'cuirass/armor' is named, و links to مغفر 'covering/veil' which is specified by هندوان ('of the Indians') — here هندوان is a genitival/attributive noun indicating origin or ownership. The phrase is nominal and functions as continuation of the inventory.
segments.s568-g77: به اندیشه is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'into/unto thought.' دل را is the definite object (را marks 'the heart') placed before the verb for emphasis. به جای آوردید is a verbal phrase ('put into place / set straight'); آورید here is an imperative/optative 2nd-person-plural ('bring/put'), addressing attendants. Overall the clause orders an action: put the heart into thought.
segments.s568-g78: خرد را ('wisdom' marked by را) is the object to be brought. بران is an adverbial particle meaning 'thereupon / with that' linking this action to the previous clause. رهنمای is a substantive 'guide / adviser', here the role to be filled by خرد; آورید is again the imperative/optative 'bring (pl)'. The clause literally commands: 'Bring wisdom, thereupon, as a guide.'
segments.s568-g79: چو is the temporal/conditional conjunction 'when/as.' از باختر marks origin 'from the west' (باختر = west), چشمه 'spring/source' is the theme, and اندر + کشید is a verbal phrase 'drew in / drew forth' with کشید as past 3rd-person singular (classical poetic form of کشیدن). The clause is temporal and sets the scene for what follows.
segments.s568-g80: شب is the grammatical subject (personified 'night'); آن چادر قار is a noun phrase 'that (dark/gloomy) tent' with the demonstrative آن and descriptive element قار following چادر. بر سر is a locative phrase 'over/on the head,' and کشید is the past 3rd-person singular verb 'drew/placed' — thus 'night drew that tent over the head.' The verb-final order and personification are typical poetic devices.
segments.s568-g9: بدو is a prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' placed before the reporting verb; گفت is the past tense 3rd-person singular 'he said' introducing direct speech; روزی is an adverbial 'one day' (time phrase). که‌ای خوب روی is a vocative phrase: که‌ای here introduces the direct address and ای (vocative) + خوبِ روی = 'O good/beautiful-faced one.' Word order is typical older Persian: indirect object / adverb + verb, then the quoted vocative.
segments.s568-g10: This is the quoted imperative/consultative speech. چه مردی is 'what sort/kind of man (are you)?' مترس is an imperative 'fear not' (negative command). ایچ is an emphatic adverb 'at all / nothing' (here: 'do not fear at all'). با من بگوی is an imperative/request 'speak with me' (بگوی is the archaic/poetic form used here to urge speech). The clause strings commands and encouragements to reply.
segments.s568-g11: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/since/that'); در چرم is a prepositional phrase 'in/within leather' (literally 'in leather'); چو is a conjunction meaning 'when' (introducing the circumstance); نازک اندام تو = 'your slender/delicate figure' (نازک modifies اندام, and تو is the possessive/2nd-person).
segments.s568-g12: همی is an adverb of continuity/intensity ('ever, continually'); بگسلد is a 3rd-person singular present/habitual form (in older Persian forms like بگسلد express present/habitual action) meaning 'it breaks/tears apart'; خواب و آرام تو = 'your sleep and repose' (direct objects), so the verb takes the two nouns as its objects: 'it continually breaks your sleep and calm.'
segments.s568-g13: چو = 'as/like' (introducing a simile); سروی = 'a cypress' (metaphorical image); بدی here is archaic/poetic and in context functions as a 2nd-person past/action 'you placed/you set' (an old verbal form used in Ferdowsi), so the clause reads 'as a cypress you set ...'; بر سرش = 'upon her head' (بر + سر + enclitic ش = 'on her'); گردماه = 'round/moon-like face' (a compound meaning 'full moon' or 'moon-faced person').
segments.s568-g14: : بران = 'upon that/thereon' (archaic demonstrative/adverb linking back to the preceding image); ماه = 'moon' (here 'moon-faced one'); کرسی = 'seat / throne' (the moon is said to become a seat); ز مشک سیاه = 'of/from black musk' (ز = 'from/of', مشک سیاه = 'black musk') — the phrase describes the moon-face becoming a throne made of black musk (a poetic ornament).
segments.s568-g15: کنون = 'now' (temporal adverb); چنبری = 'a hoop / ring' (noun subject of گشت); گشت is the past/preterite or perfective 'became / turned into' (here 'came to be'); بالای سرو = 'atop the cypress' (بالای = 'on top of', سرو = 'cypress') — the clause says a hoop/ornament came to sit upon the cypress's top.
segments.s568-g16: تن = 'the body'; پیل + وارت (وارت = variant/suffixed form of 'وار' = '-like') = 'elephant-like' (پیل‌وارت = having the bulk/character of an elephant); به کردار = 'in the manner/likeness' (به + کردار, where به marks the relation 'in/regard to' or 'in the manner of'); غرو = 'sunset/decline/waning' (here 'the waning/setting sun' — so the whole clause: 'the body, elephant-like, in the guise/appearance of the declining sun').
segments.s568-g65: یکی is an indefinite determiner meaning “a certain/one”; it precedes the noun جشن. باشد is a 3rd-person optative/subjunctive (classical Persian) used to mark existence or stipulation (“let there be” / “will be”). به روم اندرون is a prepositional phrase (به + place + اندرون) placed after the predicate to give location: “inside Rum.”
segments.s568-g66: که is a subordinating conjunction introducing a relative/explanatory clause (“at which/that”). The list مرد و زن و کودک is a coordinated subject phrase; آید is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of آمدن meaning “come,” and برون is a postverbal adverb meaning “out/forth.” Word order here is conjunction + subject list + verb + directional adverb.
segments.s568-g67: چو functions as a temporal conjunction here (“when”). کدبانو is the house‑mistress/lady; از + شهر + بیرون is a prepositional phrase marking motion out of the city, and شود (شد in subjunctive/optative 3rd pers.) is used with the directional phrase بیرون to mean “goes out / becomes outside.”
segments.s568-g68: بدان here is built from ب + دان (classical/poetic contracted form of به + آن) and functions adverbially as “on/at that” (i.e. ‘with respect to that festival’). جشن خرم is the noun + adjective (“joyful festival”), and به هامون شود uses به + place (هامون, “the plain”) plus شود (3rd pers. optative/indicative) to mean “becomes (joyful) on the plain.”
segments.s568-g69: شود is 3rd-person subjunctive/optative meaning “becomes / there is” and introduces a hypothetical result. جای خالی is a noun + adjective (“an empty/vacant place”). و connects to the speaker’s self-reference: من چاره‌جوی is a nominative phrase with من (I) followed by a descriptive noun چاره‌جوی (“one who seeks a remedy” / “problem‑solver”).
segments.s568-g70: بسازم is 1st-person singular, present/subjunctive of ساختن used to mean “I will make/arrange” (author’s promise/intent). نترسم is the negative present/subjunctive of ترسیدن prefixed by ن‑: “I do not fear.” ز is the poetic form of از meaning “from/because of.” پتیاره is an obscure/archaic word (here left uncertain) and گوی (here) appears tied to speech; the clause as a whole says the speaker will act and is not afraid of that speaker/utterance.
segments.s568-g71: This is a simple coordinated noun list: دو + nouns enumerated with و. دو اسپ = “two horses/steeds.” گوپال is a lexical item whose precise meaning is uncertain in this list (it could be a name or an occupational/title); تیر و کمان is the standard coordinated pair “arrow(s) and bow.” The enumerated items form the direct object of the following verb.
segments.s568-g72: به پیش تو is a dative/local phrase meaning “before you / in your presence” (به + پیش + pronominal تو). آرم is a 1st‑person verb form (classical) meaning “I bring / I will bring” (the form corresponds to an archaic subjunctive/optative first person; the supplied senses list did not include ‘bring,’ so sense is marked unknown). The final به روشن‌روان is a vocative or epithet applied to the addressee: به + روشن‌روان (bright‑soul).
segments.s568-g57: چنین گفت پس = 'thus he said' (چنین adverb before the verb). پس is a transitional adverb 'then/after that'. با کنیزک به راز is a prepositional phrase 'with the maidservant, in secret' where به راز functions adverbially ('in secret'). Word order is terse/poetic: adverb + verb + follow-up phrase.
segments.s568-g58: که‌ای is the vocative/addressing particle (classical written as که ای) meaning 'O you...' introducing direct address. پاک is an adjective 'pure'. بینادل is an archaic/rare adjective here modifying پاک (literary 'heart-related' / 'of noble heart'); it is an uncommon word. و نیک‌ساز is a coordinated adjective/noun phrase 'and good-doer/benefactor', completing the praise.
segments.s568-g59: یکی چاره = 'a certain remedy' (یکی used indefinitely). باید is the modal 'must/it is necessary' governing the following infinitive. کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'. ساختن is the infinitive 'to make/create' placed after باید in classical/poetic order: 'a remedy must now be made.'
segments.s568-g60: ز هر گونه اندیشه انداختن is an infinitival complement (purpose or specification of the remedy): ز 'from/of', هر گونه 'every kind of', اندیشه 'thought/worry', انداختن 'to throw/cast (away)'. Together the phrase means 'to cast away every sort of thought' (i.e., to set aside all considerations).
segments.s568-g61: که here introduces a wish/ purpose clause 'so that/in order that'. ما را uses را as the object/beneficiary marker 'for us'. گذر باشد is an optative/subjunctive construction 'may there be a passage' (باشد used as optative). از شهر روم = 'from/out of the city of Rome' (روم meaning Byzantium/the Roman city).
segments.s568-g62: مباد is a negative optative particle 'may it not be / God forbid'. آفرین here means 'praise/acclamation' (not the verbal stem). بر چنین مرز و بوم = 'upon such a border and homeland'. The whole clause is a curse/wish: 'May there never be praise for such a border and land.'
segments.s568-g63: کنیزک بدو گفت = 'the maid said to him' (subject + indirect object + verb). بدو is 'to him' (indirect object). گفت here is the past verb 'said' (the provided noun senses don't match the verbal use, so the sense index is left unspecified). فردا پگاه is a temporal adverbial 'tomorrow at dawn' placed after the verb for poetic variation.
segments.s568-g64: شوند is the subjunctive/present plural of شدن used here as an intransitive verb meaning 'they will be / they will go / they shall proceed' (classical usage places the verb before subject). این بزرگان = 'these great ones' (the nobles). سوی جشنگاه = 'toward the place of celebration'. The word order is poetically inverted (verb before subject and complement).
segments.s568-g25: نگویی is a negative/optative form (ن- + گویی) addressing 'you' and functions like 'do not say' or 'may you not say' (second-person). به بدخواه is a prepositional phrase 'to/for an ill-wisher' (the intended recipient of any telling). راز مرا is the direct object meaning 'my secret' — here the clitic م indicates possession ('my') attached to راز and the subsequent را is the definite/object marker emphasizing the noun as the object of نگویی.
segments.s568-g26: کنی is the present-subjunctive/optative form of کردن used here in a subordinate clause meaning 'you would cause/make' (i.e. 'you would bring about'). یاد is a noun 'remembrance/memory' serving as what is caused; درد و گداز مرا is a possessive phrase 'my pain and anguish' (درد = sorrow/pain, گداز = ardor/anguish). The clause therefore describes the result that would be caused by speaking the secret.
segments.s568-g27: بگویم is a first-person verbal form ('I will tell' / 'let me tell') used to introduce what follows. ترا is the clitic object 'to you'. آنچ is the relative pronoun 'that which / what', and درخواستی qualifies it as 'that which you requested' (درخواستی here functioning as the requested thing or as a nominalized/attributive form). Word order places the object pronoun after the verb and the relative phrase after آنچ.
segments.s568-g28: به گفتار is a prepositional phrase 'in/by speech / in words' specifying the medium. پیدا کنم is a compound where پیدا (apparent/manifest) precedes the verb کنم ('I make / I cause' in subjunctive/optative first person) — together 'I make ... apparent'. راستی is the object 'truth' (or 'truthfulness'), so the clause reads 'I will make the truth apparent in words.'
segments.s568-g29: کنیزک is the subject 'the little maidservant' (diminutive). به دادار سوگند خورد is an idiomatic oath: به introduces the object of the oath ('by the Creator' = دادار) and سوگند خورد (literally 'ate an oath') is the conventional compound meaning 'swore' or 'took an oath' — here خورد functions as the light verb in the idiom.
segments.s568-g30: به again introduces an oath or instrument ('by' / 'with'). زنار شماس is a genitive construction 'the sexton’s girdle' (زنار = girdle, شماس = sexton / church assistant). هفتاد گرد literally 'seventy turns' — گرد here denotes turning/about and is governed by the numeral to mean 'circled/seven times seventy (i.e. seventy circuits)'.
segments.s568-g31: به جان is a conventional oath formula 'by the life/soul of ...'; مسیحا names the invoked figure 'the Messiah' (here as the object of the oath). و links the next oath-item; سوک صلیب is 'the dirge/mourning of the cross' (سوک = mourning/dirge, صلیب = cross). Together they form the objects invoked in the speaker’s oath/adjuration.
segments.s568-g32: به continues the oath-formula ('by'). دارای ایران functions as 'the possessor/ruler of Iran' (دارای used substantively as 'holder/possessor of Iran'). گشته is the perfect/participle 'has become' (here used in a jussive/optative sense to express the hoped-for or cursed result), and مصیب means 'afflicted/stricken by calamity'. The phrase thus expresses a wish/curse that the possessor of Iran become afflicted.
segments.s568-g41: به هنگام is a temporal prepositional phrase meaning “at the time of.” نان شیرگرم is the thing expected/served (bread and warm milk), with شیرگرم acting as a compound modifier (‘warm milk’). آوری is 2nd/3rd-person present/subjunctive/optative form of آوردن used here as an exhortation/imperative sense ‘bring’ (poetic present form).
segments.s568-g42: بپوشی is the prefix be- + present/subjunctive (hortative) of پوشیدن meaning ‘cover/conceal’ and governs سخن as its object: ‘cover/guard your speech.’ The following phrase نرم نرم‌آوری repeats and intensifies softness: نرم is an adjectival/adverbial modifier ‘gentle/soft,’ and نرم‌آوری (a deverbal/nominalized form) indicates softening/appeasement — here an instruction to soften one’s words.
segments.s568-g43: به شیر اندر is a locative/ablative phrase ‘in/into milk,’ with اندر reinforcing location. آغارم is first‑person singular present (poetic) from آغار/آغاز ‘to begin’ (here ‘I begin’ / ‘I set about’). این چرم خر is the direct object (‘this donkey-hide’) placed after the verb due to poetic word order.
segments.s568-g44: که introduces a result/purpose clause (‘so that/that’). گردد is the 3rd person singular present/subjunctive of گشتن ‘become’ (optative sense ‘may become’). به گیتی سمر is a prepositional phrase ‘in the world, [the] s̲amar’ where سمر means ‘evening-talk / notable talk’ (i.e. become talked-about).
segments.s568-g45: پس از من is a temporal phrase ‘after me’ (پس here = ‘after’ with از marking separation). بسی سالیان is a quantifying phrase ‘many/much years’ (بسی = much/many) and بگذرد is 3rd person singular present/subjunctive/optative of گذشتن used to mean ‘will pass’ or ‘may pass’ in a future/prophetic sense.
segments.s568-g46: بگوید here functions as a reported/future-like verb ‘they will say’ (poetic present/subjunctive); همی is an adverb of continuity/emphasis ‘ever/still.’ هرک دارد خرد is a conditional/relative phrase ‘whoever has wisdom’ (هرک = ‘whoever/each one’ and دارد = ‘has’), standing as the understood subject of ‘they will say’ or as an elliptic comment.
segments.s568-g47: کنیزک is the accusative/desired object ‘a young maidservant.’ همی is emphatic/continuous ‘you always/just’ and خواستی is 2nd person past (poetic) of خواستن ‘you wanted/desired.’ شیر گرم (warm milk) is what she is expected to bring — object phrase placed after the verb for poetic cadence.
segments.s568-g48: نهانی is an adverb ‘secretly’; ز here means ‘from’ (poetic preposition), so نهانی ز هرکس = ‘secretly from everyone/every person.’ به آواز نرم is a prepositional phrase ‘with a soft voice,’ where آواز is the noun ‘voice’ and نرم modifies it adverbially (‘softly/gently’).
segments.s568-g33: که introduces a subordinate clause meaning "that." راز تو با کس نگویم is literal word order (راز = object "your secret", تو = possessor), با + کس functions as "to anyone" (classical use of با for the indirect object), and نگویم is a first‑person negative present/subjunctive/promise form "I do not tell/I will not tell." ز بن is a short prepositional phrase "from the root/basis" modifying the following verb phrase.
segments.s568-g34: نجویم is the first‑person verb "I do not seek" (negative with initial ن). همی is the classical continuous/affirmative particle here reinforcing the ongoing/firm character of the verb. بتری is used as a nominal idea of "severance/cutting off" (i.e., withdrawing or breaking away), and زین سخن (= از این سخن) means "from this word/pledge/saying;" together the clause means "nor will I seek to sever myself from this speech/pledge."
segments.s568-g35: همه راز شاپور is the noun phrase "all the secret(s) of Shapur" (راز شاپور = Shapur's secret). با او (classical use) marks the indirect object "to him," and بگفت is the past narrative verb "he told/said." The order is typical for classical Persian: object/possessive phrase + indirect object + verb.
segments.s568-g36: بماند is a past/resultative verb meaning "remained/was left." آن سخن is the clause's subject/topic "that speech/utterance," نیک و بد are coordinate adjectives "good and bad," and در نهفت is a prepositional phrase meaning "hidden/concealed." Together: "that speech, good and bad, remained hidden."
segments.s568-g37: بدو گفت = "he said to him" (بدو = to him/unto him; گفت here is reported speech). اکنون "now" sets the time. چو + present verb (فرمان دهی) forms a conditional/clause: چو here means "if/when" and دهی is second‑person present/subjunctive/imperative sense "you give/you command."
segments.s568-g38: بدین راز is a prepositional phrase meaning "by/with this secret" (بدین = به + این in classical orthography). من before دل functions as a possessive "my" (دل من = my heart), and گروگان دهی is a verb phrase "you give (as) a hostage/pledge" with دهی second‑person present/subjunctive. The whole clause is an instruction/offer: "by this secret, give my heart as a hostage."
segments.s568-g39: سر از بانوان برتر = "a head/position superior to the ladies" (سر = honor/position here). آید ترا is inverted classical order: verb آید "will come" followed by the clitic ترا "to you" ("will come to you"). The clause predicts elevation above the women.
segments.s568-g40: جهان زیر پای = "the world under (your) feet" (زیر پای = under the foot/under the control of). اندر آید ترا repeats the classical inversion "will come under (you)" with آید (will come/happen) and the clitic ترا (to you). Together: "the world will be under your feet."
segments.s568-g17: دل من (noun + enclitic) = “my heart,” where the possessive pronoun follows the noun. همی is the classical continuous/progressive particle placed before the predicate; بر تو indicates the target of the passion (toward you). بریان is a predicative adjective/participle meaning “burnt/consumed (by love),” and شود is the 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive auxiliary meaning “becomes / is (made),” producing the sense “is continually consumed.”
segments.s568-g18: دو چشمم = “my two eyes” (دو numeral + noun + enclitic -م for possession). شب و روز is an adverbial phrase “night and day.” گریان is a predicative adjective/participle “weeping/tearful,” and شود again is the 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive “become / are (made),” giving the meaning “my two eyes are/weep day and night.”
segments.s568-g19: بدین سختی = “in/with this hardship” (بدین is a classical prepositional phrase ‘in this’/‘with this’ formed from ب- + این). اندر چه جویی is an inverted interrogative: اندر (in/within) + چه (what) + جویی (second‑person present form of جستن ‘you seek’), and همی placed after gives a continuous/progressive sense “do you continually seek/are you always seeking.”
segments.s568-g20: که introduces the subordinate/explanatory clause “that/since.” راز تو = “your secret” (noun + possessive pronoun). با من = “with me / to me.” نگویی is the negative present/subjunctive (ن‑ + گویی) meaning “you do not say / you do not tell,” and the final همی again gives an ongoing or emphatic sense (“you still do not tell”). Word order is topic (راز تو) + complement (با من) + verb (نگویی).
segments.s568-g21: بدو = “to him” (prepositional phrase: be + indirect object). گفت is the past tense verb “said”; following it is the subject شاپور (proper name). کای is a vocative particle “O,” and خوب‌چهر is a vocative epithet “fair‑faced.” This is direct speech introduced by a verbal phrase (“to him Shapur said”).
segments.s568-g22: گرت is a conditional particle (= اگر + ت) meaning “if”/“if you” (classical conditional). هیچ here intensifies the conditional (roughly “at all / ever”). بر من = “toward me,” and بجنبید (from جنبیدن) is a present/subjunctive form agreeing with مهر as its logical subject: مهر بجنبید = “love stirs/moves.” The clause thus reads “if ever any love stirs toward me.”
segments.s568-g23: به سوگند = “by (your) oath” where به here functions instrumentally (“by/with”). پیمانت is پیمان (pledge/covenant) plus the 2nd‑person enclitic -ت (‘your’). خواهم is first‑person singular present/future “I will/wish” and یکی is the object “one (thing).” The sequence means “By your oath / by oath of yours I will request one thing.”
segments.s568-g24: کزان is a relative/adverbial “from which / that from which.” نگذری is the negative subjunctive/imperative form (ن‑ + گذری) meaning “you do not pass / you do not forsake/you will not abandon.” جاودان is an adverbial adjective “forever,” and اندکی “a little,” so the whole clause means “that you never depart from it (do not let it pass) even for a little while.”
segments.s568-g49: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'when'. The clause ends with برداشتی (past, 2nd‑person singular) 'you took/lifted'; یکی modifies جام 'a single cup'. کشتی here is anomalous in the listing and does not fit the common noun senses given (likely a poetic or corrupt form), so the clause reads 'when you took a cup'.
segments.s568-g50: بر آتش is a prepositional phrase 'upon the fire'. همی is an archaic/adverbial particle that often adds immediacy or durative sense to the verb; تیز functions adverbially 'swiftly'. بگذاشتی is past, 2nd‑person singular with the old verbal prefix بـ 'you placed/put'. The whole segment describes the immediate action following the previous clause.
segments.s568-g51: به نزدیک شاپور is a directional phrase 'to/close by Shapur'. بردی is past, 2nd‑person singular 'you carried/brought'. نهان is an adverb 'secretly' modifying the verb; word order (to/near + name + verb + manner) is typical in epic Persian.
segments.s568-g52: نگفتی here is the past 2nd‑person with negative prefix نـ implied 'you did not say/tell' (classical Persian often uses نگفتی for 'you did not tell'). نهان repeats the manner 'secretly'. با کس اندر جهان is a prepositional phrase 'to/with anyone in the world' — combined with the negation it yields 'you told no one in the world'.
segments.s568-g53: دو هفته is a duration 'two weeks'. سپهر 'the sky/heavens' is the locus for that time. اندرین 'in these (days)' reinforces the temporal setting. گشته شد is a compound perfect/passive-like phrasing (گشته: perfect participle; شد: auxiliary) meaning 'had gone/passed' — together giving 'two weeks of the sky had passed.'
segments.s568-g54: به فرجام 'at the end' or 'finally' sets a resultive time. چرم خر 'the donkey's leather/hide' is the subject. آغشته شد is a passive/result phrase 'became soaked' (آغشته 'soaked/imbued' + شد auxiliary). The clause states the eventual condition of the hide.
segments.s568-g55: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. شاپور is the subject. زان 'from that' (referring back to the skin/hide) + پوست 'skin' form the prepositional origin. آمد (past) + برون 'out' = 'came out (of it)'. The clause describes Shapur emerging from the skin.
segments.s568-g56: همه 'whole/entire' modifies دل 'heart' leading into the predicate 'دل پر از درد' — 'a heart full of pain/sorrow'. تن 'body' likewise takes پر 'full (of)' and the preposition ز/از introduces the cause 'of blood'. The structure is a balanced descriptive clause: 'whole heart full of pain and body full of blood.'
segments.s568-g1: چنین is an adverb 'thus' that frames the clause. تا here means 'until/by the time' and introduces a temporal clause; برآمد is the past form of برآمدن ('came to pass/occurred') and is the main verb of this temporal clause. برین is a poetic demonstrative built from بر + ین (a fossilized compound corresponding to 'these' / 'this'), and چندگاه is a temporal noun 'for a while/for several days'. The clause therefore literally reads 'thus, until these few days came to pass' = 'thus after a few days had passed.'
segments.s568-g2: به ایران is a prepositional phrase marking direction/location ('into/into the region of Iran'); پراگنده is an adjective 'scattered'; گشته is the perfect/participial form of شدن used here with پراگنده to express the result/state 'has become scattered'; سپاه is the subject 'the army' placed at the end in the typical SOV/poetic order.
segments.s568-g3: به روم marks place ('to/at Rome' — classical Persian uses به for location/direction). آنک is a relative/demonstrative particle 'that/whom' introducing or pointing to the following noun; شاپور را is the direct object with the object marker را; داشتی is the 2nd-person past of داشتن 'you had/kept'. The clause therefore states that the addressee had Shapur in Rome.
segments.s568-g4: شب و روز is an adverbial idiom 'night and day' meaning continuously. تنهاش is تنها + clitic -ش ('him/her alone') with the clitic marking the third-person object. نگذاشتی is the negative past ('you did not let/you did not leave'); here it functions as 'you did not abandon/leave him (alone)'.
segments.s568-g5: کنیزک is a noun 'young maidservant'. نبودی is the past negative of بودن in 2nd person 'you were not' and negates the predication. ز before شاپور is the causal/ablative use 'because of/from' linking the cause ('because of Shapur') to the predication شاد ('happy'). The structure says 'you were not a maid who was happy because of Shapur.'
segments.s568-g6: ازان literally 'of those/from that' here functions demonstratively 'one of those'. کش is an agentive/nominal element (a short-form nominalizer meaning 'one who/that person') making 'ازان کش' roughly 'one of those people'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' linking to ایرانیان 'Iranians' (the group), and بد نژاد is a compound meaning 'ill-born' or 'of bad lineage' (بد modifying نژاد). The phrase thus identifies the subject as 'one of those Iranians of base birth.'
segments.s568-g7: شب و روز again means 'night and day' (constantly). زان is 'from/for that' ('because of that'); چرم is 'leather/skin' (here likely a metonymic or poetic image). گریان is a participle/adjective 'weeping' used predicatively, and the final بدی is an archaic/paleo-orthographic copula = بودی ('you were') in past 2nd person; this poetic spelling is used to form 'you were weeping' (note: the word بدی is not listed with this meaning in the provided senses, so it is treated here as the past copula).
segments.s568-g8: دل او is 'his heart'. ز before شاپور is 'because of' or 'on account of Shapur'. بریان is an adjective/participle 'roasted/burnt/consumed' used metaphorically for a heart 'consumed (by love or grief)'. The final بدی again functions as the past copula (poetic spelling of بودی) giving 'his heart was consumed' (or 'you caused his heart to be consumed' depending on the subject); as above this verbal sense is not among the provided senses, so it is marked separately.

sentences.s568-s41: King Shapur's heart grew anxious, wondering what the little maid of dawn would do the next day.
sentences.s568-s37: He first set his heart in thought; from the manger he drew out two precious steeds.
sentences.s568-s38: That same sword and Gopāl and Bargastavān; that same cuirass and the Indians' maghfar.
sentences.s568-s39: Set the heart into thought; bring wisdom to serve as its guide.
sentences.s568-s40: When he drew the spring from the west, the night drew that dark tent over his head.
sentences.s568-s5: He said to him one day: "O fair-faced one, what sort of man are you? Do not be afraid at all — speak with me."
sentences.s568-s6: For when your delicate form is within leather, it ever breaks your sleep and rest.
sentences.s568-s7: Like a cypress you set the moon-faced one upon her head; that moon became a seat (throne) of black musk.
sentences.s568-s8: Now a ring (hoop) came to the top of the cypress; the body became elephant-like, assuming the aspect of the waning sun.
sentences.s568-s33: A certain festival will be held inside Rum, at which men, women, and children will come forth.
sentences.s568-s34: When the house‑mistress goes out of the town, that joyful festival will make the plain glad.
sentences.s568-s35: If a vacant place opens up, I — one who seeks remedies — will make (fill) it; I do not fear the petty‑tongued.
sentences.s568-s36: I will bring before you two horses, two gupāls, and bow and arrows, O Bright‑Soul.
sentences.s568-s29: Then he said thus, in secret to the maidservant: "O pure-hearted, benign doer,"
sentences.s568-s30: A certain remedy must now be made: to cast away every kind of thought.
sentences.s568-s31: so that there may be a passage for us out of the city of Rome — God forbid praise for such a frontier and land.
sentences.s568-s32: The maid said to him, 'Tomorrow at dawn these great ones will go toward the place of celebration.'
sentences.s568-s13: Do not tell my secret to an enemy, for you would make them remember my pain and anguish.
sentences.s568-s14: I will tell you what you asked for, and I will make the truth manifest in my words.
sentences.s568-s15: The little maidservant swore by the Creator — and, by the sexton’s girdle, she made seventy circuits.
sentences.s568-s16: By the life of the Messiah and the mourning of the cross, may the ruler/possessor of Iran be stricken with calamity.
sentences.s568-s21: At the time of bread, bring warm milk; veil your words and soften them gently.
sentences.s568-s22: I shall begin (treating) this donkey-hide in milk, so that this hide may become the talk of the world.
sentences.s568-s23: After me many years will pass, and people will say (it) — whoever has wisdom.
sentences.s568-s24: You desired a young maidservant who would bring warm milk; secretly from everyone, in a soft voice.
sentences.s568-s17: That I will not tell your secret to anyone, nor will I (from the root) seek to sever myself from this word.
sentences.s568-s18: He told him all of Shapur's secrets; that speech — both good and bad — remained concealed.
sentences.s568-s19: He said to him: now, if you give the command, by this secret give my heart as a hostage.
sentences.s568-s20: A head higher than the ladies will come to you; the world will come under your feet.
sentences.s568-s9: My heart is continually consumed for you; my two eyes weep day and night.
sentences.s568-s10: In such hardship what are you seeking, since you do not tell your secret to me?
sentences.s568-s11: To him Shapur said, 'O fair‑faced one, if ever any love stirs toward me...'
sentences.s568-s12: By your oath I will ask one thing: that you never abandon it even for a little while.
sentences.s568-s25: When you lifted a single cup, you promptly set it upon the fire.
sentences.s568-s26: You secretly carried it to Shapur; you told it to no one in the world.
sentences.s568-s27: Two weeks of the heavens passed; at last the donkey's hide became soaked.
sentences.s568-s28: When Shapur came out of that skin, his whole heart was full of pain and his body full of blood.
sentences.s568-s1: Thus, after a few days had passed, the army became scattered throughout Iran.
sentences.s568-s3: You were not a maid who rejoiced because of Shapur; you were one of those Iranians of base/ill birth.
sentences.s568-s4: Night and day, because of that leather, you wept; his heart, because of Shapur, was consumed (burning with passion).
